hypotheses.complete_requirement: A website where users search for a stock ticker and the system generates a basic PDF report with fundamental financial metrics (P/E, EPS, market cap) and a simple buy/hold/sell recommendation, using free data APIs, with no customization options.

An interactive web dashboard where users search stocks to view real-time charts with technical indicators, and can generate customizable reports (PDF/Excel) including both fundamental and technical analysis, with options to select analysis parameters.

A web application that, on searching a stock, generates a comprehensive narrative analysis report (as a web page) covering company overview, competitive landscape, risks, and valuation, using AI and natural language generation, possibly with sentiment analysis from news.

A report generation platform where users can define custom report templates (choosing metrics, charts, time periods), schedule automated report generation (daily/weekly) for a watchlist of stocks, and receive reports via email in PDF/Excel, with multiple data providers.

hypothesis_disagreements: type of stock analysis content (fundamental vs technical vs narrative vs custom)
report output format (PDF, web page, interactive dashboard, email)
level of user interaction and customization
update_notes: Initial hypotheses derived from common variations in stock report system requirements.

candidate_questions.question: What specific information or analysis should be included in the generated stock report?
candidate_questions.rationale: Determines the depth and type of analysis content, distinguishing between basic financials, technical indicators, narrative summaries, or fully customizable content.

possible_answers.answer: Basic financial metrics only (e.g., P/E, market cap)
Both fundamental and technical analysis
Narrative summary with company overview and valuation
User-defined customizable metrics and charts

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on basic fundamental metrics, so likely to specify financial metrics only.
H2 includes both fundamental and technical analysis as core features.
H3 produces narrative reports, so answer aligns with narrative summary.
H4 emphasizes customizable templates, so user will define metrics.

candidate_questions.question: In what format would you prefer to receive or view the generated stock analysis report?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies the delivery mechanism and user experience, distinguishing between static documents, web pages, interactive dashboards, and email delivery.

possible_answers.answer: PDF document
Interactive web dashboard
Web page with narrative content
Email delivery of document (PDF/Excel)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 basic report typically delivered as PDF.
H2's interactive dashboard is primarily web-based, but may offer PDF export.
H3 narrative report is a web page.
H4 schedules email delivery of documents.

candidate_questions.question: Should the system allow you to customize the analysis parameters before generating the report, or should it be fully automatic?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifies the need for user interactivity and configuration, ranging from zero-touch to full template-building.

possible_answers.answer: Fully automatic with no customization
Some customization (e.g., select indicators, time period)
Full template builder for custom reports

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a simple automatic generator with no options.
H2 allows selecting indicators and parameters.
H3 generates narrative automatically; minimal customization.
H4 is specifically about custom template building.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need the ability to schedule automatic report generation for multiple stocks at regular intervals?
candidate_questions.rationale: Determines the necessity of background job scheduling, multi-stock watchlists, and periodic delivery features.

possible_answers.answer: Yes
No
Maybe, would like to learn more

likelihoods.rationale: H1 lacks scheduling; likely not needed.
H2 may optionally include scheduling but not core.
H3 on-demand narrative; scheduling unlikely.
H4 explicitly requires scheduling.

candidate_questions.question: What specific metrics or indicators would you like the stock analysis to include?
candidate_questions.rationale: Distinguishes between basic fundamental focus, technical focus, narrative, or customizable metrics.

possible_answers.answer: Basic financial ratios (e.g., P/E ratio, EPS)
Technical indicators (e.g., RSI, moving averages)
Narrative or qualitative analysis (e.g., company overview, competition)
User should be able to choose from a wide range of metrics

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on basic fundamental metrics.
H2 includes both fundamental and technical indicators with some choice.
H3 emphasizes narrative analysis, possibly with selectable depth.
H4 allows full customization of metrics.

candidate_questions.question: Once a report is generated, what would you typically do with it or need to happen next?
candidate_questions.rationale: Reveals need for sharing, scheduling, automation, or simple download.

possible_answers.answer: View or download the report for personal use
Schedule the report to be generated automatically and delivered (e.g., email)
Share the report with others (e.g., via link or file)
Integrate the report into other tools or workflows

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a simple download-and-use system.
H2 could involve sharing or light scheduling.
H3 is mostly for on-screen reading.
H4 is built for scheduling and automated delivery.

candidate_questions.question: How comprehensive or detailed should the generated analysis report be?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies whether a quick snapshot, in-depth report, or customizable depth is needed.

possible_answers.answer: A quick summary/snapshot (one-page or graphical)
A detailed, multi-section report (several pages)
Customizable depth depending on my needs

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a basic, quick report.
H2 allows some depth customization.
H3 is inherently detailed.
H4 emphasizes customizable depth.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need to track multiple stocks over time, perhaps with updates or alerts?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifies if the system should support watchlists, alerts, or ongoing tracking.

possible_answers.answer: No, just one-off searches for individual stocks
Yes, a watchlist with the ability to generate reports for multiple stocks
Yes, with automated alerts or updates on specific events or changes
Yes, with portfolio tracking and performance reporting

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is typically one-off.
H2 might include a watchlist and alerts.
H3 is focused on individual in-depth analysis.
H4 supports watchlists, scheduling, and potentially portfolio features.

Professional client reporting
Educational or academic research
Other or unclear purpose

Multiple stocks in batch
Uncertain or varies

Narrative generation is computationally intensive, so typically one at a time.

possible_answers.answer: Real-time data is essential
End-of-day or delayed data is acceptable
No strong preference or not important

Narrative reports focus on analysis and news sentiment, not real-time prices.
Scheduled reports typically use end-of-day snapshots; real-time only if intraday reporting is needed.

Save search/watchlist for manual regeneration
Automated periodic report generation
Not sure or other

Central feature is scheduling recurring reports for a watchlist.

Must comply with relevant gambling regulations.

A customizable poker room system for private groups. Users create rooms with full control over settings (blinds, max players, betting structure). Play money only, integration with social media for invites, basic game records per room. Targeted at small groups and event organizers.

A competitive real-money cash-game platform (no tournaments) with leaderboards, advanced statistics, and a focus on player skill tracking. Supports standard no-limit hold'em with configurable blinds per table. Real-money deposits and withdrawals, robust account management.

Tournaments only

Predefined standard settings only

Not important or no strong preference

Not important or no strong preference

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A full real-money gambling platform with deposits/withdrawals, support for cash games and tournaments (no-limit hold'em primarily), standard table sizes, public lobby with filters, player statistics, and standard chat. Must comply with relevant gambling regulations.

A play-money social poker app for casual play among friends. Virtual chips only, no real-money transactions. Includes cash games and optional tournaments, basic chat, simple account management with friends list, and game records for fun.

A customizable poker room system for private groups. Users create rooms with full control over settings (blinds, max players, betting structure). Play money only, integration with social media for invites, basic game records per room. Targeted at small groups and event organizers.

A competitive real-money cash-game platform (no tournaments) with leaderboards, advanced statistics, and a focus on player skill tracking. Supports standard no-limit hold'em with configurable blinds per table. Real-money deposits and withdrawals, robust account management.

hypothesis_disagreements: Monetary model: real money vs play money
Game formats: cash games only vs tournaments included
Target audience: friends/small groups vs public competitive
Room customization: fixed settings vs full customization

update_notes: User expressed indifference about in-game currency purpose. No strong preference between real money and virtual chips, so probabilities remain unchanged.

candidate_questions.question: Should the platform support tournaments in addition to regular cash games, or only cash games?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes hypotheses that include tournaments (H1, H2) from those that do not (H4), and reveals whether the user envisions a competitive or casual experience.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, tournaments are important
No, only cash games
I don't have a preference / either is fine

likelihoods.rationale: H1 envisions a full gambling platform with tournaments, so user likely wants them.
H2 includes tournaments as optional; user may be indifferent or want them as a fun feature.
H3 does not explicitly mention tournaments; likely indifferent or open to optional tournaments.
H4 is strictly cash-game focused, so user would reject tournaments.

candidate_questions.question: To what extent should users be able to customize game room settings, such as blinds, number of players, and betting structure?
candidate_questions.rationale: This targets the room customization dimension, separating the fully customizable private rooms (H3) from more standardized approaches (H1, H2, H4).

possible_answers.answer: Full customization, users can set all parameters
Limited customization, some presets but not fully configurable
Standard settings only, no customization needed
No preference

likelihoods.rationale: H1 likely has limited customization with public lobby filters, but not full room-by-room control.
H2 might allow some settings for casual groups, but not full customization.
H3 is centered on fully customizable rooms; user would demand full control.
H4 might allow configurable blinds per table, but not full room customization.

candidate_questions.question: How detailed should the game records and player statistics be? For example, basic win/loss tracking or comprehensive hand histories and analytics.
candidate_questions.rationale: This clarifies the depth of statistics needed, distinguishing competitive data-heavy platforms (H1, H4) from casual fun-oriented ones (H2, H3).

possible_answers.answer: Detailed statistics, hand histories, analytics
Basic win/loss and maybe some simple stats
Just minimal tracking, like number of games played
No preference

likelihoods.rationale: H1 includes player statistics, so detailed records are expected.
H2's game records are for fun, so basic stats suffice.
H3 tracks basic room-level records, not deep analytics.
H4 has advanced statistics and leaderboards, so detailed records are key.

candidate_questions.question: Should the lobby be public for anyone to join any table, or should it be possible to create private invite-only rooms?
candidate_questions.rationale: This determines the platform's openness, separating private group play (H3) from public competitive or social play (H1, H2, H4).

possible_answers.answer: Public lobby, all tables visible to all users
Private invite-only rooms only
Both public and private rooms available
No preference

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes a public lobby with filters, essential for real-money gambling.
H2 is social, likely public, but may allow private tables for friends.
H3 is entirely about private invite-only rooms.
H4 is competitive and public, likely with a lobby for all players.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple lead generation website with static service pages, an embedded third-party credit score widget, and a basic contact form that sends email notifications. No CRM integration, no user accounts, and client provides all content.

A professional lead generation website with custom API integration for credit score checks, a multi-step lead capture form that integrates with a CRM like Salesforce, and a polished design with provided branding assets. Content primarily provided by client with minor copywriting assistance.

An educational content-heavy website with blog articles, downloadable guides, and a newsletter signup. The credit score feature is a link to an external service, and the lead form simply emails the company. Content will require significant copywriting and SEO optimization.

A full-service portal where users can register, check their credit through an integrated credit monitoring service, track credit repair progress, and communicate with agents. The lead capture automatically creates user accounts and initiates cases, with heavy emphasis on secure data handling and compliance.

update_notes: Initial belief state based on brief requirement.

candidate_questions.question: What specific functionality do you expect for the credit score check feature on the website?
candidate_questions.rationale: The implementation of the credit score feature significantly differentiates hypotheses, ranging from a simple embedded widget to a full credit monitoring portal.

possible_answers.answer: Simple embedded widget from a third-party service
Custom API integration with a credit bureau
Link to an external free credit score website
Full credit monitoring dashboard with user accounts

likelihoods.rationale: H1 favors a simple, low-effort solution like an embedded widget.
H2 requires a custom, integrated credit check via API.
H3 leans toward a simple external link due to focus on content.
H4 demands a full dashboard with credit monitoring capabilities.

candidate_questions.question: What should happen when a visitor submits their information through the lead capture form?
candidate_questions.rationale: The processing of form submissions indicates the depth of integration and whether user accounts or CRM are needed.

possible_answers.answer: Send an email notification to a company address
Store submissions in a database with an admin panel
Integrate directly with a CRM like Salesforce
Create a user account and start a credit repair case

likelihoods.rationale: H1 prefers a straightforward email notification.
H2 needs CRM integration for lead management.
H3 may use email and possibly newsletter signup.
H4 requires automatic user account creation and case initiation.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple web-based CRM for customer service with basic customer records (name, email, phone), call logging with timestamp and free-text notes, full history viewable by all authenticated users, and two roles: admin (user management) and agent (log/view calls). No telephony integration.

An advanced call center system with detailed customer profiles (including purchase history and support tier), automatic call logging with duration and recording, searchable history by date/customer/agent, role-based access with admin, supervisor, and agent roles where supervisors can view reports and performance metrics, and computer-telephony integration (CTI).

A lightweight collaboration-oriented call tool focusing on team workflows: minimal customer info (name, company), call logs with notes, tags for issue type and resolution, history for auditing, simple manager vs. staff access with assignment capabilities, possibly a mobile app, and no integration with external systems.

An enterprise-grade customer service platform with full CRM integration (contacts, accounts, opportunities), detailed call logging with custom fields and categorization, advanced search and analytics on history, fine-grained permissions including field-level security and SSO, and compliance features like audit trails.

hypothesis_disagreements: access control granularity and role structure
integration with external systems (e.g., phone, CRM)
update_notes: Initialized four diverse hypotheses covering a range from simple to enterprise-grade solutions based on the user's high-level need.

An advanced call center system with detailed customer profiles (including purchase history and support tier), automatic call logging with duration and recording, searchable history by date/customer/agent, role-based access with admin, supervisor, and agent roles where supervisors can view reports and performance metrics, and computer-telephony integration (CTI).

A lightweight collaboration-oriented call tool focusing on team workflows: minimal customer info (name, company), call logs with notes, tags for issue type and resolution, history for auditing, simple manager vs. staff access with assignment capabilities, possibly a mobile app, and no integration with external systems.

An enterprise-grade customer service platform with full CRM integration (contacts, accounts, opportunities), detailed call logging with custom fields and categorization, advanced search and analytics on history, fine-grained permissions including field-level security and SSO, and compliance features like audit trails.

candidate_questions.question: What specific customer information do you need to manage in the system?
candidate_questions.rationale: The required depth of customer data will help distinguish between a basic contact manager, a detailed profile store, or a full CRM integration.

possible_answers.answer: Basic contact details only (e.g., name, email, phone)
Detailed profile including purchase history and support tier
Minimal information like name and company
Full CRM data integrated with contacts, accounts, and opportunities

likelihoods.rationale: H1 expects basic contact information as the core model.
H2 requires detailed profiles including purchase history to support tiered service.
H3 focuses on lightweight usage with minimal customer data.
H4 assumes integration with an existing CRM and thus full CRM data availability.

candidate_questions.question: What details should be captured when logging a customer service call?
candidate_questions.rationale: The level of detail in call logs distinguishes between simple note-taking, automated system data capture, or structured categorization.

possible_answers.answer: Timestamp and free-text notes
Automatic duration and recording with some structured fields
Tags for issue type and resolution
Custom fields and detailed categorization

likelihoods.rationale: H1 uses simple free-text notes.
H2 includes automatic data capture and likely structured fields for reporting.
H3 emphasizes tagging for collaboration and tracking.
H4 requires detailed categorization and custom fields for enterprise analytics.

candidate_questions.question: What levels of access control and user roles do you envision?
candidate_questions.rationale: The granularity of access control indicates the system's complexity and the need for hierarchical or fine-grained permissions.

possible_answers.answer: Two roles: admin and agent
Three roles: admin, supervisor, agent
Simple manager vs. staff with assignment capabilities
Fine-grained roles with field-level permissions and SSO

likelihoods.rationale: H1 has basic two-role structure.
H2 introduces a supervisor role for reporting and management.
H3 matches a lightweight manager/staff division.
H4 requires fine-grained enterprise access control and SSO.

candidate_questions.question: Do you require integration with any existing systems, such as a phone system or CRM?
candidate_questions.rationale: Integration needs strongly separate standalone tools from those embedded in a larger ecosystem.

possible_answers.answer: No integration needed
Integration with a phone system (CTI)
Integration with a mobile app only
Integration with both CRM and phone system

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is standalone.
H2 requires CTI; CRM integration may be separate.
H3 may be mobile-focused but standalone or no phone integration.
H4 integrates deeply with CRM and likely phone for full enterprise suite.

candidate_questions.question: What customer information do you need to store and manage in this system?
candidate_questions.rationale: This reveals the complexity of the customer data model, distinguishing between basic contact info, moderate additional fields, and detailed CRM-like profiles.

possible_answers.answer: Basic contact details only (name, phone, email)
Moderate information (including company, notes, and perhaps a few custom fields)
Detailed profiles (purchase history, support tier, linked accounts, extensive custom fields)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 expects very basic customer info.
H2 includes advanced profiles with purchase history and tier.
H3 leans towards minimal info but may allow some extra fields for context.
H4 involves comprehensive CRM data with many custom fields.

candidate_questions.question: How should call logs be created in the system?
candidate_questions.rationale: This clarifies whether call logging is manual, automatic (via telephony integration), or a hybrid, which separates simple logging tools from advanced CTI solutions.

possible_answers.answer: Completely manual entry by agents
Automatic logging when a call occurs (via phone system integration)
Both manual and automatic logging supported

likelihoods.rationale: H1 lacks telephony integration, so logging is manual.
H2 includes CTI for automatic logging with duration/recording.
H3 is collaboration-focused, likely manual but could have optional auto-logging.
H4 may integrate with phone systems but also allow manual overrides and custom logging.

candidate_questions.question: What search or filtering capabilities do you expect when looking back at call histories?
candidate_questions.rationale: This uncovers needed history retrieval complexity, from basic listing to advanced search with analytics, which affects system design.

possible_answers.answer: Simple list or basic search by date and customer
Search by multiple fields (agent, tags, issue type) and maybe keyword
Advanced search with filters, analytics, and possibly report generation

likelihoods.rationale: H1 only provides basic view of history.
H2 includes search by date/customer/agent and performance reports.
H3 likely has simple tagging and filtering but not advanced analytics.
H4 demands advanced search and analytics with compliance features.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need this system to integrate with any existing tools or telephony systems?
candidate_questions.rationale: Integration requirements strongly differentiate standalone solutions from those needing CTI or CRM sync.

possible_answers.answer: No integrations needed
Needs to integrate with phone system (CTI)
Needs to integrate with other business tools (CRM, email, etc.)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is standalone with no integrations.
H2 requires CTI integration; possibly also CRM but primarily phone.
H3 avoids external integrations for simplicity.
H4 integrates with CRM and possibly phone, but CRM is primary.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple web-based CRM for customer service with basic customer records (name, email, phone), call logging with timestamp and free-text notes, full history viewable by all authenticated users, and two roles: admin (user management) and agent (log/view calls). No telephony integration.

An advanced call center system with detailed customer profiles (including purchase history and support tier), automatic call logging with duration and recording, searchable history by date/customer/agent, role-based access with admin, supervisor, and agent roles where supervisors can view reports and performance metrics, and computer-telephony integration (CTI).

A lightweight collaboration-oriented call tool focusing on team workflows: minimal customer info (name, company), call logs with notes, tags for issue type and resolution, history for auditing, simple manager vs. staff access with assignment capabilities, possibly a mobile app, and no integration with external systems.

An enterprise-grade customer service platform with full CRM integration (contacts, accounts, opportunities), detailed call logging with custom fields and categorization, advanced search and analytics on history, fine-grained permissions including field-level security and SSO, and compliance features like audit trails.

candidate_questions.question: What customer information would you like to store and manage?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifying the customer data model will distinguish between basic contact-only systems and those requiring detailed profiles or CRM integration.

possible_answers.answer: Basic contact details (name, phone, email)
Extended profiles (company, role, interaction history)
Unsure or no strong preference

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes minimal customer records, so basic details are very likely; unlikely to need extended profiles.
H2 requires detailed customer profiles for context and routing, so extended info is expected.
H3 focuses on lightweight collaboration, often with minimal customer data; slight chance of wanting more details.
H4 integrates full CRM with accounts and opportunities, so extended profiles are essential.

candidate_questions.question: How do you plan to use the call history? What kind of search or filtering do you need?
candidate_questions.rationale: The user's view on history interaction is unknown; a simple chronological list vs. advanced search will separate low-end from high-end solutions.

possible_answers.answer: Just view all calls in chronological order
Basic search by date or customer
Advanced filtering and analytics (by agent, keyword, outcome)
Not sure

likelihoods.rationale: H1 provides a simple view of all calls; basic search is possible but advanced analytics unlikely given its lightweight scope.
H2 includes role-based search and reporting, so demanding advanced search is anticipated, but basic search might also be acceptable.
H3 leans toward simple auditing, so chronological view is most plausible; some might need basic filters for assignments.
H4 emphasizes advanced analytics and full-text search, making rich filtering very likely.

candidate_questions.question: Beyond a description, what other details would you need to record for each call?
candidate_questions.rationale: The previous answer only mentioned a description; uncovering if additional fields (e.g., timestamp, caller ID, resolution) are needed will clarify the required call logging depth.

possible_answers.answer: Nothing else, only the description
Automatically captured meta-data (timestamp, caller number, duration)
Categorization, resolution status, or custom fields
Don't know

likelihoods.rationale: H1 expects minimal manual entry, but timestamps are usually implicit; user may not consider them separate, so 'only description' is most probable.
H2 relies on automatic logging and categorization for reporting, so meta-data and resolution status are expected.
H3 keeps logging simple but may use tags; equal chance for minimal vs. slightly more structured entries.
H4 demands customizable fields and detailed categorization for compliance and analytics.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of reports or analytics, if any, would you need from the call data?
candidate_questions.rationale: Assessing the need for reporting will separate systems that focus on operational call logging from those that require performance monitoring and business intelligence.

possible_answers.answer: None, just view individual calls
Basic summaries (e.g., call volume per agent)
Detailed metrics and dashboards (e.g., resolution time, customer satisfaction trends)
Unsure

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a bare-bones CRM with no emphasis on analytics, so 'none' is very likely.
H2 includes supervisor-level performance dashboards, making detailed metrics the primary expectation.
H3 may need basic team workload reports but is primarily a communication tool; often remains without analytics.
H4 targets enterprise analytics, so detailed dashboards and metrics are a core requirement.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple poll website where anyone can create a single poll with multiple options, and anyone can vote without registration. Votes are anonymous, and results are shown after voting. No user accounts.

A community poll platform where only admins can create polls, and all community members (authenticated) can vote. Real-time results are visible to all members. Voting is identified.
A poll website that allows registered users to create polls that can be embedded on external sites. Voting is anonymous and does not require login. Results are private to the poll creator.

candidate_questions.question: Who should be able to see the voting results, and at what point should they become visible?
candidate_questions.rationale: This addresses the unresolved result visibility dimension and helps distinguish between hypotheses where results are public after voting, real-time, or private to creator.

possible_answers.answer: Everyone sees results after they vote
Results are visible in real-time to all visitors
Only the poll creator can view results
Results are shown after the poll closes to all users

likelihoods.rationale: H1 expects results after voting, so A1 is most likely.
H2 has results visible to all; timing could be after voting or real-time, but not private.
H3 states real-time results visible to all members, so A2 is most likely.

candidate_questions.question: How should the system handle a user voting multiple times on the same poll?
candidate_questions.rationale: This probes whether authentication is required and if votes are restricted to one per user, distinguishing hypotheses with login requirements from those without.

possible_answers.answer: Allow unlimited votes (no restriction)
Prevent multiple votes by requiring user login
Prevent multiple votes using browser cookies/IP without login

likelihoods.rationale: H1 has no registration, so likely allow multiple votes, but might use cookies to limit.
H2 requires login, so must prevent multiple via login.
H3 has authenticated members, so login restriction.
H4 anonymous voting without login, so likely unlimited or cookie-based.

candidate_questions.rationale: This directly addresses voting anonymity, a key disagreement. Answers will differentiate systems where votes are anonymous from those where they are identified.

possible_answers.answer: Completely anonymous, no link to user identity
Votes are linked to user identity and visible to poll creator
Votes are linked to user identity and visible to all
Anonymous to public, but identifiable by poll creator

likelihoods.rationale: H1 explicitly says votes are anonymous.
H2 voting is identified, so likely visible to creator or all.
H3 identified, community might see who voted.
H4 anonymous voting, but results private to creator, so maybe creator can see individual? But says anonymous, so likely A1.

Username and password login required
Email address required with verification

likelihoods.rationale: H1 requires no registration.
H2 needs user accounts, likely username/password.
H3 community members likely have accounts.
H4 anonymous voting, so no login to vote, but creators register. So for accessing poll? Could be no identification for voters.

candidate_questions.question: Should voting be anonymous, or should it be linked to a user’s identity?
candidate_questions.rationale: Directly addresses the unresolved dimension of voting anonymity, separating H1/H4 (anonymous) from H2/H3 (identified).

possible_answers.answer: Anonymous voting
Identified (non-anonymous) voting
No preference / doesn’t matter

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes anonymous voting, so a user with this true requirement would likely prefer anonymity.
H2 requires identified voting, so the answer would favor identification.
H3 also involves identified community members, so identical to H2.
H4 has anonymous voting and no login, so anonymous answer is expected.

candidate_questions.question: Should voters be required to have an account and log in before they can vote?
candidate_questions.rationale: Probes the voter authentication dimension, distinguishing H2/H3 (login required) from H1/H4 (no login).

possible_answers.answer: Yes, login required
No, no login required
No preference

likelihoods.rationale: H1 does not require accounts, so answer likely 'no login'.
H2 depends on user accounts, so a 'yes' answer is expected.
H3 also requires authenticated community members.
H4 allows voting without login, so 'no login' is likely.

candidate_questions.question: How should the system prevent the same person from voting multiple times in one poll?
candidate_questions.rationale: The answer may reveal whether the user envisions account-based tracking (H2/H3) or unrestricted anonymous voting (H1/H4).

possible_answers.answer: Require login and record votes per account
Use browser/cookie/IP restrictions without login
No prevention needed / allow multiple votes
No preference

likelihoods.rationale: H1 lacks accounts, so prevention would likely be via technical means or not at all; slightly more chance of A2 or A3.
H2 already has accounts, so login-based tracking is natural.
H3 follows the same pattern as H2.
H4 without login may lean toward technical prevention or none; similar to H1.

candidate_questions.question: Should the voting process be open to the general public without any registration, or should it be restricted to a specific group of registered users?
candidate_questions.rationale: Combines authentication and scope in one question, effectively separating public (H1/H4) from gated (H2/H3) systems.

possible_answers.answer: Open to everyone, no registration needed
Restricted to registered users
No preference

likelihoods.rationale: H1 has no registration, so open access expected.
H2 limits voting to logged-in users, so restricted answer.
H3 restricts to community members.
H4 allows anonymous voting without login, so open.

Full marketplace would include map search capabilities.

filter by price, beds, baths, location

yes, a map view is essential

yes, allow making offers or initiating transactions online

A modern AI-powered email productivity suite with features like smart compose, email summarization, follow-up reminders, read receipts, undo send, and unified inbox for multiple accounts. Emphasizes personal efficiency with advanced organization tools (snooze, labels, scheduled sending).

candidate_questions.question: What types of emails do you primarily plan to send with this platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: Distinguishes personal correspondence from business communications and bulk marketing campaigns.

possible_answers.answer: Personal one-to-one or small group emails
Business correspondence and internal team communications
Mass email campaigns to subscribers

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is purely personal, so almost certainly one-to-one.
H2 is business-focused, so mainly internal and external business correspondence.
H3 is marketing-oriented, so almost exclusively mass campaigns.
H4 mixes personal and professional productivity, but unlikely to handle bulk marketing.

candidate_questions.question: How important are team collaboration features, such as shared inboxes or delegated access, for your platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: Separates individual-use tools from team-oriented business platforms.

possible_answers.answer: Critical – shared inboxes and delegation are must-haves
Somewhat important – basic sharing might be useful
Not important – this is for individual use only

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a personal client, so collaboration is largely irrelevant.
H2 is business-oriented; team features are central.
H3 marketing tools often have multi-user roles, but collaboration is not primary.
H4 focuses on individual productivity; team features are secondary.

candidate_questions.question: Do you require advanced AI-assisted writing features like smart suggestions, email summarization, or automated follow-up reminders?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifies if the platform is a cutting-edge AI productivity tool or a more conventional email client.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, advanced AI features are critical
Only basic features like spellcheck; no advanced AI
No AI features are needed

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is basic; AI is not expected.
H2 businesses may want some AI, but not primary.
H3 may use AI for subject lines, but core is templates.
H4 is defined by AI productivity features.

candidate_questions.question: How do you expect to manage contacts and subscriber information?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies whether it is a simple address book, a CRM, or marketing list management.

possible_answers.answer: Simple personal address book with basic details
Detailed CRM with profiles and segmentation
Subscriber list management for email campaigns
Not sure yet

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is personal, so a basic address book.
H2 often needs CRM integration for business contacts.
H3 centers on subscriber list management.
H4 may use personal contacts or integrate with existing services; user might be uncertain.

candidate_questions.question: Who will be the primary users of this email platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: This question targets the target audience dimension, which is a key disagreement among hypotheses (individual vs. team vs. organization).

possible_answers.answer: Just myself
A small team
A large organization
A mix or other

likelihoods.rationale: Personal email clients are almost always used by individuals.
Team collaboration features strongly imply small teams or organizations.
Email marketing can be done by individuals or teams, often in a marketing department.
AI-powered productivity features are typically aimed at individual professionals.

candidate_questions.question: What are the most important features you need in an email platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: This open-ended question allows the user to express their core needs, which will directly point to one hypothesis over others based on feature priorities.

possible_answers.answer: Basic composing, sending, and organizing
Team collaboration and sharing features
Bulk sending and campaign management
Smart AI assistance and productivity tools

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on basic functionality, though some users might also want modest AI features.
H2's primary value is team collaboration; other features are secondary.
Email marketing is defined by bulk sending and campaign tools.
AI-powered features are the core differentiator for H4.

candidate_questions.question: Would you need the ability to send bulk emails to many recipients at once, such as for newsletters or marketing campaigns?
candidate_questions.rationale: Bulk sending is a key distinguishing feature of email marketing platforms; a definitive answer will strongly support or rule out H3.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, I need to send bulk emails
No, it is primarily for one-to-one emails
Maybe occasionally
I am not sure

likelihoods.rationale: Personal email clients rarely require bulk sending.
Business collaboration platforms may occasionally need bulk send, but it is not core.
Bulk sending is essential for email marketing.
AI productivity tools focus on one-to-one communication; bulk sending is atypical.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple personal email client for composing, sending, and organizing personal emails, with basic contact management and folder organization. Integrates with standard email protocols (IMAP/SMTP). No team features, bulk sending, or advanced AI.

A business-oriented email platform with team collaboration features like shared inboxes, delegated access, email templates, and integration with calendars and tasks. Designed for organizational use with admin controls.

An email marketing platform for creating and sending bulk promotional emails, managing subscriber lists, and tracking campaign performance. Includes template designers, A/B testing, and analytics. Focused on marketers, not daily one-to-one correspondence.

A modern AI-powered email productivity suite with features like smart compose, email summarization, follow-up reminders, read receipts, undo send, and unified inbox for multiple accounts. Emphasizes personal efficiency with advanced organization tools (snooze, labels, scheduled sending).

candidate_questions.question: Can you describe the typical number of recipients for an email you would send?
candidate_questions.rationale: This should clarify whether the use case is personal (one-to-one), small group/business, or bulk marketing, distinguishing all hypotheses.

possible_answers.answer: Typically one recipient
A few recipients (e.g., small team)
Large lists of recipients
It varies or not important

likelihoods.rationale: Personal email typically one-to-one, rarely small groups.
Business emails often to small teams, sometimes one-to-one, occasionally small bulk.
Marketing emails are bulk by definition.
AI-powered productivity suits lean personal, but could be used for small groups; rarely bulk.

candidate_questions.question: Will you be the only person using this platform, or do you need to share access or collaborate with others on emails?
candidate_questions.rationale: Collaboration requirement strongly differentiates business team use from personal/marketing (marketing may have shared access but not typically per-email collaboration).

possible_answers.answer: I will be the only user
I need to share access with a team
I might need collaboration features but not sure
Not important or I don't know

likelihoods.rationale: Personal use is solo.
Business platform needs team sharing.
Marketers may have individual accounts or small team access, but often not real-time per-email collaboration.
AI suites focus on individual productivity, minimal collaboration.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need any advanced features like AI-powered writing assistance or email scheduling?
candidate_questions.rationale: AI capabilities are a key differentiator for the AI-powered suite hypothesis, and also help distinguish from simpler clients.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, AI writing help would be useful
I need scheduling and reminders but not AI writing
No, I just need basic composing and sending
I'm not sure or it's not important

likelihoods.rationale: Simple client rarely has AI or scheduling.
Business tools may have scheduling, some AI nowadays, but not central.
Marketing platforms focus on templates and analytics, not typically AI writing.
AI suite's core value prop includes smart compose, summarization.

I need shared contacts and team organization
I manage large subscriber lists with segmentation
I don't manage contacts heavily or it's not important

likelihoods.rationale: Personal: basic address book.
Business: shared contacts and team organization.
Marketing: large subscriber lists.
AI suite may have advanced personal contact management but not shared or bulk.

candidate_questions.question: In what ways, if any, do you expect to collaborate with others on email composition or management?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly probes the need for team features (shared inboxes, assignment, commenting), distinguishing the business collaboration hypothesis (H2) from others.

possible_answers.answer: Describes specific team collaboration needs (e.g., shared inboxes, assigning emails, commenting)
No collaboration needed, purely personal use
Other / vague response

likelihoods.rationale: Simple personal client unlikely to include team features; user would say no.
Business platform strongly expects team collaboration; user would describe some coordination needs.
Marketing platforms sometimes support multiple users, but often single marketer; likely no strong collaboration beyond maybe list sharing.
Personal productivity suite rarely involves team management; user would state no collaboration.

candidate_questions.question: What insights or analytics, if any, would you like to see about the emails you send?
candidate_questions.rationale: Targets marketing analytics (H3) vs basic read receipts (H2/H4) vs none (H1), helping gauge the depth of performance tracking needed.

possible_answers.answer: Requests detailed analytics (open rates, click-throughs, A/B testing)
Only basic delivery/read confirmation, or no analytics needed
Other / vague response

likelihoods.rationale: Personal client unlikely to have analytics; user expects nothing.
Business may need some tracking (e.g., read receipts), but not full campaign analytics typically.
Marketing platform core feature is analytics; user would likely request detailed metrics.
Productivity suites may offer read receipts and basic tracking, but not advanced analytics; user may want some but not full marketing analytics.

candidate_questions.question: How do you envision managing your incoming emails and staying organized? For example, would you prefer automatic sorting, labels, or maybe search-based organization?
candidate_questions.rationale: Explores the user's organization preferences, distinguishing AI-driven automatic features (H4) from manual folder-based approaches (H2) or simple search (H1).

possible_answers.answer: Prefers advanced AI-driven organization (auto-categorization, priority inbox, smart filtering)
Prefers manual organization with folders or rule-based filtering
Unclear / other

likelihoods.rationale: Basic client usually has folders, not AI.
Business email often relies on manual rules and folders; AI not typical core feature.
Marketing platforms focus on outbound, inbound organization less emphasized; likely manual or basic.
AI productivity suite's key value prop is smart organization; user would expect this.

candidate_questions.question: Can you describe the typical number of recipients for your emails? Do you usually send to individuals, small groups, or large mailing lists?
candidate_questions.rationale: Directly assesses sending scale, which is a key differentiator: marketing bulk sends (H3) versus one-to-one or small group communication (H2/H4).

possible_answers.answer: Mostly individuals or small groups (less than ~50 recipients)
Often large mailing lists (hundreds or thousands of recipients)
Other / mixed / unclear

likelihoods.rationale: Personal use implies low volume, individuals.
Business communication is usually with individuals or smaller teams, occasional larger announcements.
Marketing is defined by bulk sending to large lists.
Productivity suite is for personal or small business, not mass marketing.

candidate_questions.question: What types of driving jobs should the platform primarily support?
candidate_questions.rationale: This will help determine if the focus is on commercial trucking, general driving, or gig delivery, which distinguishes H1, H2, and H3.

possible_answers.answer: Commercial truck driving only (e.g., long-haul, CDL required)
All types of driving jobs (truck, taxi, delivery, ride-sharing)
Primarily on-demand delivery or ride-sharing gigs
Other or unspecified

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is specifically about commercial truck drivers, so A1 is most likely.
H2 covers all driving jobs, so A2 is expected.
H3 is gig-focused, so A3 is most likely.
H4 is full-service logistics, it could cover various types, but might lean towards commercial; somewhat ambiguous.

candidate_questions.question: How do you envision employers primarily finding suitable drivers on the platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: This clarifies the core interaction model: whether employers post jobs, search driver profiles, or rely on automated matching.

possible_answers.answer: Employers post job listings and drivers apply
Employers search a database of driver profiles
Automated matching algorithm suggests drivers to employers
Combination or other

likelihoods.rationale: For niche commercial trucking, job posting is typical.
General site might have multiple methods.
Gig platforms often use automatic matching or offers.
Full-service might offer all.

candidate_questions.question: What additional services or features beyond basic job matching should the platform include?
candidate_questions.rationale: This addresses the need for extras like background checks, which differentiates H4 from others.

possible_answers.answer: Background checks and license verification
Training resources or certifications
Rating/review system for drivers and employers
No additional services, just basic matching

likelihoods.rationale: Commercial trucking often requires compliance features.
General site might keep it simple.
Gig apps may have ratings but often minimal extras.
Full-service includes many extras.

candidate_questions.question: Should the platform focus more on full-time employment, part-time, or gig/contract work?
candidate_questions.rationale: This distinguishes gig-focused H3 from employment-focused others.

possible_answers.answer: Primarily full-time employment
Mix of full-time and part-time
Primarily gig/contract/freelance work
All types equally

likelihoods.rationale: Commercial driving is mostly full-time.
General site covers all.
Gig work is core.
Full-service could have all.

candidate_questions.question: How do you envision the employment arrangement for drivers on the platform?
candidate_questions.rationale: This will clarify whether the platform should focus on full-time, gig, or both, which strongly distinguishes the gig-oriented hypothesis (H3) from others.

possible_answers.answer: Primarily full-time, permanent positions
Primarily flexible gig/on-demand work
Both full-time and gig opportunities
Any type, as long as it connects drivers to jobs

likelihoods.rationale: Commercial truck driving is almost exclusively full-time.
General platform might support all arrangements, with a slight tilt toward mixed.
Gig platform is heavily focused on flexible, short-term work.
Logistics industry has both, but full-time is more common.

candidate_questions.question: What level of integration with external services (e.g., load boards, background check providers) do you need?
candidate_questions.rationale: This directly probes the need for industry-specific integrations, a key differentiator between the niche compliance-heavy H1/H4 and the simpler H2/H3.

possible_answers.answer: High integration with industry-specific services (e.g., load boards, DOT compliance)
Moderate integration with common services like background checks
Minimal integration, just a standalone platform
No requirement or not sure

likelihoods.rationale: Trucking niche requires load boards and DOT compliance.
General platform likely avoids heavy industry integration.
Simple gig platform needs minimal integration.
Full-service logistics likely includes background checks and possibly load boards.

candidate_questions.question: How should employers interact with drivers' applications or profiles?

An algorithm matches drivers to jobs automatically
Drivers are notified of relevant jobs, and employers can review profiles
A comprehensive system with reviews, ratings, and direct messaging

likelihoods.rationale: Trucking platforms often use job board model with direct applications.
General site could have matching but direct posting is common.
Gig app emphasizes notifications and quick review.
Full-service likely includes reviews and multiple interaction channels.

Full-service logistics includes compliance and training.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A static informational website with a list of courses (titles/descriptions), downloadable PDF learning materials for self-study, and a simple contact form. No user accounts or interactivity beyond form submission.
A dynamic site with a course catalog, user registration and course enrollment, learning activities like video lectures and quizzes, a discussion forum, and email/support ticket contact.

A full learning management system with course enrollment, payment processing, interactive modules (videos, quizzes, hands-on labs), progress tracking, and multiple contact options (live chat, phone, email, booking).

A marketing-oriented site with course previews, demo learning activities (sample quiz/video), no persistent accounts or enrollment, and prominent contact options (form, live chat) for lead generation.

hypothesis_disagreements: Presence and depth of user accounts and enrollment functionality
Interactivity level of learning activities (static downloads vs. interactive modules vs. demos)
Contact method complexity (single form vs. multi-channel support)
update_notes: Initial hypotheses generated from user's broad statement about courses, learning activities, and contact.

candidate_questions.question: What specific types of learning activities are you envisioning for the website? Could you describe the interactive elements or formats you have in mind?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies the nature of learning activities, which distinguishes between static materials, interactive quizzes, hands-on labs, or demo-only content.

possible_answers.answer: Static downloadable materials (PDFs, readings)
Interactive elements like quizzes and video lectures
Hands-on labs, simulations, or exercises
Sample or demo activities without full course access

likelihoods.rationale: H1 assumes only static materials.
H2 focuses on video lectures and quizzes.
H3 includes hands-on labs as a key feature.
H4 provides demo/sample activities for marketing.

candidate_questions.question: Could you describe the process you'd like for users to engage with the courses—for instance, do you anticipate users needing to register for an account, enroll in specific courses, or track their progress?
candidate_questions.rationale: Addresses the presence and depth of user accounts, enrollment, and progress tracking, which is a key disagrement among hypotheses.

possible_answers.answer: No registration needed; open access to all content
Basic account creation for course enrollment
Full student profiles with progress tracking and potentially certificates
Only a contact/interest form; no persistent accounts

likelihoods.rationale: H1 has no accounts, so B1 is most likely.
H2 needs basic accounts for enrollment.
H3 requires robust student profiles and tracking.
H4 may offer open previews but primarily uses contact forms.

candidate_questions.question: When you say 'get in touch with the institute,' what specific contact methods or features do you have in mind? For example, do you imagine a simple form, live chat, phone support, or something else?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies the complexity of contact functionality, another differentiating factor.

possible_answers.answer: A simple contact form
Live chat support
Multiple channels: email, phone, chat, and possibly appointment scheduling
Prominent click-to-chat or pop-up with minimal form; focus on immediate engagement

likelihoods.rationale: H1 typically has only a basic contact form.
H2 might include email and ticket support, moderate complexity.
H3 expects multi-channel robust support.
H4 emphasizes live chat or pop-ups for quick engagement.

candidate_questions.question: What is the primary goal of the website for the institute—is it mainly to attract and inform prospective students, or to serve as a full educational platform for enrolled students?
candidate_questions.rationale: Distinguishes between a marketing-oriented site and a comprehensive learning management system.

possible_answers.answer: Primarily to attract new students and provide course information
Mainly a platform for enrolled students to access course content and interact
Both equally; a hybrid that promotes courses and delivers education

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is essentially a brochure site, so D1 fits.
H2 leans toward an educational platform with some marketing.
H3 is a full LMS, so D2 is highly likely.
H4 is marketing-oriented, so D1 is its core.

candidate_questions.question: Who will be the primary users of this website?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifying the primary user group will help determine whether the website is targeted at the general public, researchers, journal staff, or association members, which fundamentally shapes the feature set.

possible_answers.answer: General public with an interest in medical research
Medical researchers, clinicians, and academics
Journal editors and administrative staff
Members of a specific medical association

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is reader-centric, so the primary users are the general public, but some authors may be researchers.
H2 serves authors/reviewers/editors, so medical researchers and journal staff are the main users, with some public readership.
H3 is a collaborative platform for medical professionals, so researchers are the primary users.
H4 is for association members, so members are the vast majority of users.

candidate_questions.question: What specific tasks should be included in 'managing your involvement with the journal'?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifying the meaning of 'involvement' will distinguish between manuscript submission, peer review duties, membership payments, or community participation.

possible_answers.answer: Submitting manuscripts and tracking their review process
Participating as a peer reviewer or editor
Managing membership subscriptions and payments
Collaborating with other researchers through discussions and forums

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on subscription management for readers and manuscript submission for authors.
H2 covers full editorial workflow, so submission and review are equally important.
H3 emphasizes collaboration and peer review participation.
H4 is membership-driven, so managing membership is the primary task, with minor involvement in submissions/review.

candidate_questions.question: How will users access the medical articles on this website?
candidate_questions.rationale: The access model (open, subscription, membership) is a key architectural decision and directly affects which hypothesis is most plausible.

possible_answers.answer: Open access, freely available to anyone
Behind a subscription paywall
Restricted to members of a medical association
Available for purchase on a per-article basis

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a subscription-based reader platform.
H2 provides public access to published articles.
H3 likely offers open access to foster collaboration.
H4 restricts articles to association members.

candidate_questions.question: What additional features beyond reading articles do you envision for the website?
candidate_questions.rationale: The presence of features like personalization, discussion forums, editorial tools, or membership management helps pinpoint the overall system purpose.

possible_answers.answer: Personalized reading lists, alerts, and bookmarks
Discussion forums and collaboration tools
Editorial and peer review management system
Membership profile, event registration, and payment handling

likelihoods.rationale: H1 prioritizes personalization features with some author-facing editorial tools.
H2 centers on editorial and peer review system, with minor reader features.
H3 focuses on discussion and collaboration, possibly integrating peer review.
H4 requires robust membership management features.

candidate_questions.question: What does 'managing your involvement' with the journal mean to you?
candidate_questions.rationale: Directly targets the ambiguous 'manage my involvement' requirement, which is central to distinguishing user roles.

possible_answers.answer: Submitting articles and tracking their publication status.
Handling peer review assignments as a reviewer or editor.
Managing my subscription, membership, or account details.
Participating in discussions or commenting on articles.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 includes author submission and subscription management; answers likely focus on those.
H2 centers on editorial workflow; A1 and A2 are most probable.
H3 emphasizes peer review and community discussion.
H4 is membership-focused; managing involvement primarily means membership management.

candidate_questions.question: Who should be able to read the published articles?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies the access model, which varies between open access (H1, H3) and membership/subscription (H4) models.

possible_answers.answer: Anyone, without any restrictions.
Some articles require a subscription or membership.
Only paying subscribers or members can access them.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is open-access, but might have premium features; A1 is most likely.
Academic journals often have hybrid models; A2 is common.
Collaborative platforms typically favor open access.
Membership sites normally restrict access to paying members.

candidate_questions.question: Will you need to submit articles or manage submissions on the site?
candidate_questions.rationale: Determines whether the user intends to be an author or editor, distinguishing H1/H2 from H3/H4.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, I plan to submit articles as an author.
Yes, I need to manage submissions as an editor or administrator.
No, I will only read or discuss articles.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 includes author submission; A1 is likely but A3 also possible if user is primarily a reader.
H2 supports full editorial workflow; both A1 and A2 are plausible.
H3 focuses on discussion; submissions are not core.
Membership site; submissions are unlikely unless it's an association journal.

candidate_questions.question: Should the website include features for discussing or collaborating on articles?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifies whether community or social features are needed, a key differentiator for H3.

possible_answers.answer: Yes, discussion forums or comments are essential.
No, just basic reading and access is sufficient.
Maybe light annotation or sharing features, but not a full community.

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is reader-centric with personalization, not heavy on community.
Academic management systems rarely have built-in discussion; focus is on workflow.
Collaboration is central to H3; discussion features are highly likely.
Membership sites may have forums but often not core; A2 plausible.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A website where users search for a stock ticker and the system generates a basic PDF report with fundamental financial metrics (P/E, EPS, market cap) and a simple buy/hold/sell recommendation, using free data APIs, with no customization options.

An interactive web dashboard where users search stocks to view real-time charts with technical indicators, and can generate customizable reports (PDF/Excel) including both fundamental and technical analysis, with options to select analysis parameters.

A web application that, on searching a stock, generates a comprehensive narrative analysis report (as a web page) covering company overview, competitive landscape, risks, and valuation, using AI and natural language generation, possibly with sentiment analysis from news.

A report generation platform where users can define custom report templates (choosing metrics, charts, time periods), schedule automated report generation (daily/weekly) for a watchlist of stocks, and receive reports via email in PDF/Excel, with multiple data providers.

report output format (PDF, web page, interactive dashboard, email)
level of user interaction and customization

candidate_questions.question: What specific information or analysis should be included in the generated stock report?
candidate_questions.rationale: Determines the depth and type of analysis content, distinguishing between basic financials, technical indicators, narrative summaries, or fully customizable content.

possible_answers.answer: Basic financial metrics only (e.g., P/E, market cap)
Both fundamental and technical analysis
Narrative summary with company overview and valuation
User-defined customizable metrics and charts

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on basic fundamental metrics, so likely to specify financial metrics only.
H2 includes both fundamental and technical analysis as core features.
H3 produces narrative reports, so answer aligns with narrative summary.
H4 emphasizes customizable templates, so user will define metrics.

candidate_questions.question: In what format would you prefer to receive or view the generated stock analysis report?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies the delivery mechanism and user experience, distinguishing between static documents, web pages, interactive dashboards, and email delivery.

possible_answers.answer: PDF document
Interactive web dashboard
Web page with narrative content
Email delivery of document (PDF/Excel)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 basic report typically delivered as PDF.
H2's interactive dashboard is primarily web-based, but may offer PDF export.
H3 narrative report is a web page.
H4 schedules email delivery of documents.

candidate_questions.question: Should the system allow you to customize the analysis parameters before generating the report, or should it be fully automatic?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifies the need for user interactivity and configuration, ranging from zero-touch to full template-building.

possible_answers.answer: Fully automatic with no customization
Some customization (e.g., select indicators, time period)
Full template builder for custom reports

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a simple automatic generator with no options.
H2 allows selecting indicators and parameters.
H3 generates narrative automatically; minimal customization.
H4 is specifically about custom template building.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need the ability to schedule automatic report generation for multiple stocks at regular intervals?
candidate_questions.rationale: Determines the necessity of background job scheduling, multi-stock watchlists, and periodic delivery features.

possible_answers.answer: Yes
No
Maybe, would like to learn more

likelihoods.rationale: H1 lacks scheduling; likely not needed.
H2 may optionally include scheduling but not core.
H3 on-demand narrative; scheduling unlikely.
H4 explicitly requires scheduling.

candidate_questions.question: What specific metrics or indicators would you like the stock analysis to include?
candidate_questions.rationale: Distinguishes between basic fundamental focus, technical focus, narrative, or customizable metrics.

possible_answers.answer: Basic financial ratios (e.g., P/E ratio, EPS)
Technical indicators (e.g., RSI, moving averages)
Narrative or qualitative analysis (e.g., company overview, competition)
User should be able to choose from a wide range of metrics

likelihoods.rationale: H1 focuses on basic fundamental metrics.
H2 includes both fundamental and technical indicators with some choice.
H3 emphasizes narrative analysis, possibly with selectable depth.
H4 allows full customization of metrics.

candidate_questions.question: Once a report is generated, what would you typically do with it or need to happen next?
candidate_questions.rationale: Reveals need for sharing, scheduling, automation, or simple download.

possible_answers.answer: View or download the report for personal use
Schedule the report to be generated automatically and delivered (e.g., email)
Share the report with others (e.g., via link or file)
Integrate the report into other tools or workflows

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a simple download-and-use system.
H2 could involve sharing or light scheduling.
H3 is mostly for on-screen reading.
H4 is built for scheduling and automated delivery.

candidate_questions.question: How comprehensive or detailed should the generated analysis report be?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies whether a quick snapshot, in-depth report, or customizable depth is needed.

possible_answers.answer: A quick summary/snapshot (one-page or graphical)
A detailed, multi-section report (several pages)
Customizable depth depending on my needs

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a basic, quick report.
H2 allows some depth customization.
H3 is inherently detailed.
H4 emphasizes customizable depth.

candidate_questions.question: Do you need to track multiple stocks over time, perhaps with updates or alerts?
candidate_questions.rationale: Identifies if the system should support watchlists, alerts, or ongoing tracking.

possible_answers.answer: No, just one-off searches for individual stocks
Yes, a watchlist with the ability to generate reports for multiple stocks
Yes, with automated alerts or updates on specific events or changes
Yes, with portfolio tracking and performance reporting

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is typically one-off.
H2 might include a watchlist and alerts.
H3 is focused on individual in-depth analysis.
H4 supports watchlists, scheduling, and potentially portfolio features.

candidate_questions.question: How would you like to interact with the stock analysis report?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies whether the user expects a static document, an interactive dashboard, a narrative article, or a customizable template, which strongly distinguishes the hypotheses.

possible_answers.answer: static document (e.g., PDF, printable report)
interactive web dashboard with charts and controls
narrative web page or article
customizable template where I pick sections and formats

likelihoods.rationale: H1 is a basic PDF, so static document most likely.
H2 is an interactive dashboard, so dashboard answer greatly favored.
H3 delivers a narrative web page, so narrative article most likely.
H4 emphasizes customizable templates, so template answer highest.

candidate_questions.question: What kind of data timeliness do you require for the stock analysis? For example, do you need live, real-time market data, or is end-of-day information acceptable?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies whether the system must support real-time data feeds, which impacts architecture and distinguishes dashboards from delayed reports.

possible_answers.answer: real-time/live data
delayed/end-of-day data
both are acceptable or no preference

likelihoods.rationale: Basic free data APIs are typically delayed, so delayed answer favored.
Interactive dashboards usually require real-time data.
Narrative reports often use delayed data, but real-time sentiment may be used.
Custom schedules may work with delayed data, but users could request real-time for watchlists.

candidate_questions.question: Would you need the ability to customize the report content, such as selecting which financial metrics or time periods to include?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies the level of customization, distinguishing simple static reports from fully configurable platforms.

possible_answers.answer: no customization, a standard report is fine
some customization (choose from predefined options)
yes, full customization (define what to include)

likelihoods.rationale: H1 offers no customization, so standard report highly likely.
Dashboard may allow selecting indicators, so some customization expected.
Narrative reports are typically standard, though some AI parameters possible.
H4 is all about custom templates, so full customization strongly favored.

candidate_questions.question: Do you require the system to automatically generate reports for a list of stocks on a schedule, or is it sufficient to generate a report whenever you manually search a stock?
candidate_questions.rationale: Clarifies whether automation and scheduling are needed, which separates H4 from the others.

possible_answers.answer: only manual on-demand report generation
scheduled automatic reports for a watchlist
both capabilities are needed

likelihoods.rationale: Basic search-and-generate report, no scheduling needed.
Interactive dashboard with on-demand generation, scheduling unlikely.
Narrative report on-demand, scheduling not mentioned.
H4 explicitly includes scheduled report generation, so A2 or A3 most likely.

candidate_questions.question: What is the primary purpose of generating these stock reports?
candidate_questions.rationale: The purpose strongly correlates with feature requirements: personal investing suggests simpler tools while professional reporting implies scheduling, templates, and possibly audit trails.

possible_answers.answer: Personal investment decisions
Professional client reporting
Educational or academic research
Other or unclear purpose

likelihoods.rationale: Simple PDF reports align well with personal use.
Interactive dashboards can serve both personal active traders and professional analysts.
Narrative reports are often used for deep-dive personal or educational purposes.
Scheduled, template-based reports are typical for professional/client reporting.

candidate_questions.question: Would you need to generate reports for multiple stocks at once, or only one at a time?
candidate_questions.rationale: Batch processing suggests a more systematic tool with watchlist/portfolio support, while single-stock on-demand leans toward a simpler lookup tool.

possible_answers.answer: Single stock at a time
Multiple stocks in batch
Uncertain or varies

likelihoods.rationale: Basic lookup tools typically handle one ticker at a time.
Dashboards may allow viewing multiple stocks simultaneously but reports are often per-stock.
Narrative generation is computationally intensive, so typically one at a time.
Scheduled reporting platforms inherently support batch processing for a watchlist.

candidate_questions.question: How important is it that the reports use live real-time data versus end-of-day or delayed data?
candidate_questions.rationale: Real-time data needs indicate a more technically demanding system with streaming APIs, while delayed data suffices for many fundamental analysis use cases.

possible_answers.answer: Real-time data is essential
End-of-day or delayed data is acceptable
No strong preference or not important

likelihoods.rationale: Simple fundamental reports often use end-of-day data.
Interactive dashboards with technical indicators require real-time data.
Narrative reports focus on analysis and news sentiment, not real-time prices.
Scheduled reports typically use end-of-day snapshots; real-time only if intraday reporting is needed.

candidate_questions.question: Would you like to save your search criteria or have a watchlist to auto-generate reports periodically, or is each report generated on demand?
candidate_questions.rationale: Automation and persistence requirements sharply distinguish an ad-hoc tool from a scheduled reporting platform.

possible_answers.answer: On-demand only, no saving or scheduling
Save search/watchlist for manual regeneration
Automated periodic report generation
Not sure or other

likelihoods.rationale: No customization or account implies on-demand only.
Dashboards may save recent searches but rarely include scheduled report generation.
Typically a one-shot narrative generation per request.
Central feature is scheduling recurring reports for a watchlist.

How would you like to interact with the stock analysis report?
Would you like to save your search criteria or have a watchlist to auto-generate reports periodically, or is each report generated on demand?
update_notes: User's repeated indifference to format and interactivity reduces likelihood of highly interactive (H2) and narrative (H3) reports. H1 and H4 now most probable, but key uncertainties about customization, scheduling, and data timeliness remain.

hypotheses.complete_requirement: A simple website where users search for a stock ticker and receive a static PDF report containing predefined fundamental metrics (P/E, EPS, market cap), daily market trends, and basic financial data, with no customization or scheduling.

An interactive web dashboard where users search stocks to view real-time charts with technical indicators, and generate customizable reports (PDF/Excel) with option to include both fundamental and technical analysis; no scheduling.

A web application generating, upon stock search, a comprehensive narrative analysis report (web page) with company overview, competitive analysis, AI-driven commentary, and sentiment analysis from news, with no custom templates.

A report generation platform where users can define custom report templates (choosing metrics, charts, time periods), schedule automated report generation for a watchlist, and receive reports via email/export, using multiple data providers with real-time or delayed data options.
